---
title: "Exporting dplyr Animations as GIFs with Gapminder"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Exporting dplyr Animations as GIFs with Gapminder}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = TRUE,
  message   = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  comment   = "#>"
)

can_gif <- requireNamespace("webshot2", quietly = TRUE) &&
  requireNamespace("magick", quietly = TRUE) &&
  requireNamespace("gapminder", quietly = TRUE)

gif_dir <- "../gifs"
if (can_gif && !dir.exists(gif_dir)) dir.create(gif_dir, recursive = TRUE)
```

## 1. Introduction

This vignette shows the package's GIF export path with the classic
`gapminder::gapminder` dataset. It follows the same workflow as the other
vignette: build an animation object first, then export it with
`animate_save_gif()`.

The example emphasizes a log10 transformation so the mutate step has a clear
numeric story rather than just a trivial copy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Load the data

```{r load-pkg}
library(animatedplyr)
library(dplyr)
library(gapminder)
```

```{r load-data}
gapminder_df <- as.data.frame(gapminder)
dplyr::glimpse(gapminder_df)
```

The dataset is a good fit for the three verbs because it has:

-   character and factor-like fields such as `country` and `continent`.
-   a time variable in `year` for filtering.
-   numeric measures like `lifeExp`, `pop`, and `gdpPercap` for mutation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. `select` → GIF

This export keeps the columns most useful for a quick reading of the data:
country, continent, year, and GDP per capita.

```{r select-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
sel_anim <- animate_select(
  gapminder_df, country, continent, year, gdpPercap,
  seed = 1
)

animate_save_gif(sel_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_select.gif"))
```

```{r select-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_select.gif"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. `filter` → GIF

This filter keeps only more recent observations, so the animation has a clear
before/after story.

```{r filter-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
filt_anim <- animate_filter(
  gapminder_df, year >= 1997,
  seed = 7
)

animate_save_gif(filt_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_filter.gif"), fps = 0.8)
```

```{r filter-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_filter.gif"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. `mutate` → GIF

This is the main transformation requested for the vignette: a log10 mutation of
GDP per capita.

```{r mutate-gif, eval=can_gif, results="hide"}
mut_anim <- animate_mutate(
  gapminder_df,
  pop_log10 = log10(pop),
  seed = 3
)

animate_save_gif(mut_anim, file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_mutate_log10_pop.gif"))
```

```{r mutate-embed, echo=FALSE, eval=can_gif, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(gif_dir, "gapminder_mutate_log10_gdp.gif"))
```

The source column `gdpPercap` is highlighted first, then the new
`gdpPercap_log10` column appears with the transformed values.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 6. Further Reading

-   `?animate_save_gif` for the GIF export API.
-   `?animate_select`, `?animate_filter`, `?animate_mutate` for the three verb
    animators.
-   `gapminder::gapminder` for the dataset documentation.
